author: @hootsmin
text: I wouldn't be certain, but I think Joe Prusa might be inclined to send you a Prusa SLS-1 if we told him you were going to use it to do experiments which involve adding alchemic powders to the mix, it has a tilting bed mechanism which will shake the resin and the powder in between each layer.  Really looking forward to seeing what you do with some carbon or graf een  in the mix.

author: @DigitalArtisan77
text: You're really into your material science now, the best I'd got for engineering material vs cost is an (allowed) blend of suraya tech blue and tenacious for ductility. Ambient temperature very critical but less so for smaller units. There is an open source resin chamber heater about but I don't think unless a cold shop needed.

author: @chrismalcomson2824
text:
I had a giggle when you talked about the area being clear and clean! I do car bodywork for a living and my printer lives in my workshop kitchen. It's always cluttered up with junk and a layer of dust! In fact, to use it, I have to tidy the area and use a blow gun to get rid of the dust. Having said that, it does work flawlessly somehow.

My printer was something I knocked together before you could buy ready made printers. It started off with an angle iron frame (Dexion shelving) and has evolved over the years into something resembling a Prusa i3.

text: C’mon Rob, Christmas is coming and I’m still sat on the fence (toilet actually)……please show us what it will do. 👍🙂

replies.author: @singularitysquaredllc.895
replies.text: You'll love it: also there are newer plant based resins that you should have a look at.

replies.author: @singularitysquaredllc.895
replies.text: @chrispollard6568 if it's a pi$$ing contest vs omniscient you, dissuading someone from their goals, you lose. I have both resin and FDM for ohhh... more than a decade. As for what I've printed you can't afford to know doubtless, even after the NDA you'd have to sign.

replies.author: @singularitysquaredllc.895
replies.text: @chrispollard6568 wow... you act as though it was me who claimed to do the "impossible" a classic case of projection. What "task" did Karl list that a resin printer wasn't capable of that a FDM is? None... as he didn't even list a task: wait till you take your meds before you post.

replies.author: @singularitysquaredllc.895
replies.text: @chrispollard6568 lol, no Mr. "impossible" that's how you showed you were out of your depth... In the next video Robert gives his *use cases* none of them are "gaming characters." As none of mine, are: our collective focus is on *fidelity and orders of magnitude higher precision* of build: you have it exactly backwards, there are things you can do with with a resin printer that one CAN'T do with FDM as Robert shows in the next video in the series. Ergo, *your guessing isn't valid enough to stop someone from entering the next level of this craft* certainly not because it's "messy." The precision of prototyping, and engineering, calls for resin.
